cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: The codebase has multiple significant boundary violations: bidirectional coupling between root and hermes_cli, tools reaching up to the CLI layer for configuration, documented architecture contracts contradicted by runtime imports (tools/registry.py), and widespread private symbol access across module boundaries. While the gateway/platforms hierarchy and tools/interrupt signaling show intentional, clean design, the three massive hub files (cli.py, run_agent.py, gateway/run.py) amplify coupling and make changes to shared symbols ripple unpredictably. The pervasive sys.path.insert pattern (30+ files) reflects a project that has outgrown its ad-hoc import infrastructure.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The score reflects significant structural boundary debt at the codebase level. A developer making changes would encounter recurring boundary violations and bidirectional coupling that make it hard to predict change impact. The tools->cli.py upward dependency and the registry's violated contract are high-confidence issues any senior engineer would flag. However, localized areas (gateway/platforms hierarchy, interrupt module, config types) show intentional architecture, preventing a lower score. This lands between 60 (significant drag) and 70 (mixed) -- the bidirectional coupling patterns and hub file concentration push it toward the lower end.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has a sound domain-aligned package structure: gateway/, tools/, hermes_cli/, agent/, cron/, and honcho_integration/ each own a clear capability, and within those packages decomposition is generally clean (e.g., one file per platform adapter, one file per tool, explicit __init__.py exports). However, the three largest files (run_agent.py, cli.py, gateway/run.py) are 4-6K line monoliths that concentrate multiple responsibilities, undermining the otherwise good package-level decomposition. The project structure documentation in AGENTS.md is stale -- it contains a phantom directory and omits many real modules. Research/RL code (batch_runner, trajectory_compressor, rl_cli, environments/) coexists at the top level with production agent code without clear boundary markers.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: Package-level structure is domain-aligned and a new engineer could navigate the top-level directories confidently (pulling toward 80+). But three monolithic files totaling 15K lines blur ownership within their respective domains, and the inaccurate AGENTS.md means the 'map' of the codebase cannot be trusted. Research code intermixed at the root adds cognitive overhead. These issues create enough friction that a developer would regularly encounter 'why is this here?' moments, placing this in the 70-75 range -- mixed quality where good structure is undermined by concentrated files and documentation drift.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase follows conventions with strong consistency in the areas that matter most: naming (snake_case throughout), type strategy (dataclass only), tool registration (registry.register pattern), and platform adapter structure (shared ABC with matching check_*_requirements functions). The few outliers are localized — a split in sys.path.insert usage among sibling platform adapters and one abbreviated function name — rather than systemic drift. The large tools/__init__.py barrel export is a minor convention outlier since it is rarely used by actual consumers.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: A developer can generally see a pattern in one area and trust it holds elsewhere. The tool registration, parser, and platform adapter patterns are all consistent and well-documented. The sys.path.insert split in gateway/platforms/ is the most notable inconsistency — it affects sibling modules in the same directory with no apparent rationale — pulling the score below 90. The check_ha_requirements naming outlier is minor but real. Overall this is solid with a few isolated rough edges, placing it near the 85 anchor.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The codebase has a deliberate and consistent error strategy at the tool-calling boundary (JSON error returns) and the platform adapter layer (SendResult). However, a pervasive pattern of broad 'except Exception: pass/log' throughout the gateway, CLI, and session management layers creates unpredictable error behavior. A developer working in these areas cannot tell which failures are silently swallowed, which are logged at what severity, and which propagate -- because the same pattern is used for both expected optional-dependency failures and unexpected programming errors.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The tool boundary and adapter layer show intentional error design, preventing this from being truly poor. But 139 bare except-and-pass blocks across 47 files, mixed print/logger usage for errors, and broad catches that mask programming bugs alongside IO errors represent significant drag. A developer making changes to session management or config loading cannot predict how errors propagate and would need to read each handler individually. This sits between the 60 (significant drag) and 70 (mixed) anchors, pulled up slightly by the well-designed tool and adapter error conventions.

naming_quality.dimension_character: The codebase has a strong foundation of domain-aligned naming across its core subsystems -- gateway, agent, and tools layers use specific, intent-revealing names for classes, predicates, and constants. The main friction points are concentrated in two files: skills_hub.py uses a 'do_' prefix convention that strips domain context from action names, and web_tools.py uses 'process' as a verb for what is actually content summarization. These are isolated pockets rather than systemic patterns.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Names are generally precise enough that a developer can predict behavior from names alone in most modules. The gateway layer, session management, and tool registry demonstrate consistently strong naming. The score lands at 82.5 because the do_ prefix pattern (8 functions in skills_hub.py) and the generic 'process_content_with_llm' represent recurring vocabulary drift from the codebase's otherwise domain-specific norms -- enough to occasionally slow a developer down but not enough to undermine trust in names overall.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: The codebase generally favors direct, domain-aligned abstractions -- the tool registry pattern is clean, base classes have genuine extension pressure with multiple concrete implementations, and utils.py is small and focused rather than a generic dumping ground. However, the AIAgent constructor's ~40-parameter surface acts as an implicit configuration bag that accumulates features without structural boundaries, and the tools/__init__.py barrel export is largely redundant with the registry-based system. Duplicated cache utilities in the platform base show missed opportunities for abstraction where it would actually reduce code.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase is solid but uneven from an abstraction fitness perspective. Core patterns (tool registry, platform adapter hierarchy, environment base class) are well-designed and direct. The score is held back primarily by the AIAgent parameter bag which represents a recurring friction point for any developer constructing or modifying agent instances, and by the redundant tools/__init__.py barrel. These are not severe structural problems but represent enough inconsistency that a developer encounters 'why is this different?' moments. Anchoring at 76: mostly well-served but with recurring friction from the wide constructor and redundant re-export layer.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The dependency set is reasonably well-organized with good use of optional extras for platform-specific packages and deferred imports for optional libraries. However, there is meaningful dependency drift: an unused core dependency (jinja2), three dependencies for a missing subproject bundled into core, and dual HTTP clients (requests and httpx) used for the same purpose across different modules. The requirements.txt convenience file has also diverged from the canonical pyproject.toml.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The optional extras structure and deferred imports represent genuine positive design that would push toward 85+. However, the unused jinja2 dep, three orphaned mini-swe-agent deps in core, dual HTTP clients, and requirements.txt drift collectively represent the kind of 'why is this here?' friction that pulls toward the 80 anchor. The issues are not severe enough to materially hinder evolution (60 anchor), but a developer would encounter enough confusion to question trust in the dependency manifest, landing near 78.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase is sharply split: smaller utility and client modules exhibit clean, proportionate internals with flat control flow and clear intent. However, the gateway runner -- the central message processing pipeline -- contains a monolithic _handle_message method exceeding 700 lines with deep nesting and inline config loading boilerplate repeated across many sibling methods. This unevenness means a developer working in gateway/run.py faces substantially more friction than elsewhere.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The positive patterns in utilities, data classes, and client modules are genuine strengths that serve developers well. However, the gateway runner's monolithic message handler with 5-level nesting, duplicated config loading boilerplate (11 occurrences), and the patch parser's repeated flush blocks represent significant low-level craft issues concentrated in high-traffic code paths. This pulls the score below 70 -- a developer would regularly encounter local complexity in the most important file that makes individual functions harder to follow than they should be.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has strong integration seams at its outer boundaries -- the platform adapter abstraction, control API, tool registry, and hook system are all clean, explicit, and well-separated. However, the central orchestration layer (GatewayRunner) is where these good boundaries converge into tangled, monolithic methods. Config reading is scattered across 20+ independent parse-and-extract sites for the same file, temporary agent construction is repeated with ad-hoc parameter variations, and the core message pipeline mixes every concern into a single 700-line method. A developer working at the platform or tool boundary would find quality strong; a developer modifying the gateway's core orchestration would encounter significant glue-code entropy and tangled back-and-forth patterns.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The outer integration seams (adapters, control API, tool registry, hooks) are genuinely well-designed and would anchor the score at 80+. But the gateway orchestration core -- where all these seams meet -- has 20 repeated config parses, a monolithic _handle_message method, and inconsistent temporary-agent construction patterns. These are not isolated rough edges; they represent systemic glue-code entropy at the most critical integration point. The score lands around 62, reflecting significant drag on a developer working at the central orchestration layer, per the 60-anchor ('seam design is tangled; a developer making a cross-module change must understand surprising implicit contracts').

test_strategy.dimension_character: The test suite has strong foundations — a well-designed conftest with isolation and timeout guards, behavior-based assertions rather than snapshot overuse, and thorough coverage of newer features like autoreply. However, several of the highest-traffic production modules (gateway/run.py, tools/terminal_tool.py, hermes_cli/main.py, hermes_cli/auth.py, all 7 environment backends) have zero or minimal test coverage, representing over 15,000 lines of untested core logic. The widespread object.__new__ pattern for constructing test doubles of GatewayRunner creates structural fragility — any attribute added to GatewayRunner requires updating 9 test files.
test_strategy.score_rationale: A developer making changes to the gateway runner pipeline, terminal tool, CLI entrypoints, or environment backends would have no test safety net. While the test suite covers many leaf modules well and has good infrastructure, the gaps are in the most critical, highest-traffic files. This puts the score near the 60 anchor ('meaningful risk goes unvalidated') with some lift from the strong coverage in other areas and the solid test infrastructure.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase has a strong foundation of API coherence, particularly in its platform adapter interface where all 8 adapters faithfully implement the abstract base class with identical send/connect/disconnect signatures. The ToolRegistry and HermesControl client provide clean, predictable interfaces. However, there are isolated inconsistencies in the media cache utilities (different parameter semantics for similar functions) and some wide parameter lists at internal boundaries that create minor friction.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The platform adapter hierarchy is exemplary — a developer seeing one adapter can predict all others. The tool registration and control API surfaces are similarly predictable. The score is pulled down from ~90 by the cache utility inconsistency (developers must check which cache function they're calling to know what the second param means), the wide build_source signature, and the use of untyped dict bags for autoreply config. These are localized issues in an otherwise coherent surface, placing this solidly above 80.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: Authorization in this codebase is architecturally strong: a single centralized enforcement point (_is_user_authorized in GatewayRunner) applies a consistent multi-layer auth chain across all six messaging platforms. The pairing system adds defense-in-depth with proper security controls. The control API surface is appropriately scoped to localhost with opt-in activation. The only friction a developer would encounter is that the allowlist env var names are repeated as string literals across setup, config, and migration code rather than being defined as shared constants.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The auth patterns are uniformly applied with clear, documented exceptions (HomeAssistant bypass, control API localhost binding). A developer reviewing security would find a trustworthy, centralized enforcement model. The magic-string repetition of env var names is a minor maintainability concern that prevents a score above 90, but it does not create a security gap since the enforcement point is singular. This places the codebase solidly in the 'strong with minor friction' range.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase is largely free of AI-generated noise in its core modules -- module docstrings are informative, comments explain non-obvious constraints, and logging is purposeful rather than ceremonial. However, two modules (toolsets.py and toolset_distributions.py) exhibit textbook LLM-hallmark patterns: verbose Args/Returns docstrings on trivial one-line functions, restating comments, and stale probability comments that contradict their actual values. The duplicated .env loading boilerplate across 7+ files is a cross-cutting instance of copied-verbatim code that a human would have extracted into a shared helper.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The core codebase (gateway, agent/, tools/registry, hermes_state, hermes_time) is clean and purpose-driven, which would support a score in the high 80s. However, toolsets.py and toolset_distributions.py have concentrated docstring bloat and restating comments that a developer must wade through, and the stale mismatched comments in toolset_distributions.py are a reliability concern. The 7-file .env loading duplication adds a boilerplate tax that pulls the score into the high 70s -- a developer working here would encounter these patterns regularly in certain areas but find most of the codebase respectably clean.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase has several active migration zones — most notably the SQLite/JSONL dual-write for session transcripts and the legacy toolset name mapping — but they are consistently well-documented with clear comments explaining the 'why'. Deprecated symbols are marked but sometimes linger as dead parameters or fallback paths. The overall impression is of migrations that were started with clear intent but not fully concluded, leaving a moderate amount of old+new coexistence that a developer would need to navigate.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase earns credit for clearly documenting every backward-compat layer and having tests for legacy paths (e.g. TestLegacyToolsetMap, test_legacy_root_max_turns). However, the SQLite/JSONL dual-write is a subsystem-level incomplete migration with no timeline, the deprecated on_auto_reset parameter persists in the public API, and deprecated env vars are still read at runtime. These create moderate 'which pattern should I follow?' friction, placing this solidly in the 80-85 range.

package_organization.dimension_character: The top-level package decomposition is sound — agent/, gateway/, hermes_cli/, tools/, and supporting packages map clearly to distinct domains, and gateway/platforms/ demonstrates good subdirectory design. However, the two largest production packages (tools/ at 39 files, hermes_cli/ at 26 files) are flat with mixed concerns, and the root directory accumulates peripheral scripts that share themes but are not grouped. A developer familiar with the codebase navigates well at the top level but must scan long file lists within tools/ and hermes_cli/.
package_organization.score_rationale: The clear top-level domain separation and correct placement of high-fan-in core files pull the score above the 60 threshold. However, the tools/ flat directory (39 files with identifiable subgroups like skills_*, infrastructure, and media tools), hermes_cli/ flat directory (26 files mixing subcommands, config, and display), and root-level stragglers represent recurring structural mismatches that place this firmly in the 70-range — a developer encounters enough placement friction to question where to find or add things within the two largest packages.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The codebase shows deliberate awareness of initialization coupling risks in its core architecture: the tool registry has an explicit import chain designed to prevent circularity, hermes_constants.py is intentionally dependency-free, and expensive resources use lazy initialization patterns. However, the gateway subsystem relies on sys.path manipulation at import time across 6+ modules, and the two entry points (cli.py, gateway/run.py) perform significant environment mutation at module scope, which creates fragility for tests that import these modules.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The score sits at 82.5 because the core tool/agent architecture is well-designed for import safety with documented patterns, but the gateway subsystem's reliance on sys.path.insert and the entry-point environment mutation at module scope represent recurring friction points. A developer working in the gateway or writing tests against it would encounter import-order sensitivity. The stale-snapshot constants in model_tools.py are a minor maintenance risk. These issues prevent a score above 85 but don't represent a systemic problem since the main agent/tool import chain is clean.

design_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase shows a split personality on design coherence. Peripheral subsystems (tool registry, tool call parsers, agent submodules, platform adapters) demonstrate disciplined extraction and data-driven patterns. But the three central orchestration files (run_agent.py at 5725 LOC, cli.py at 5162 LOC, gateway/run.py at 4067 LOC) concentrate too many responsibilities and contain repeated structural patterns -- especially the config.yaml loading duplication and the monster run_conversation method. The gateway's media cache triplication in base.py is another clear example of structural repetition that should be abstracted.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The positive patterns in the tool/parser registries and agent submodule extraction show the team knows how to do focused design, but the three largest files in the codebase all suffer from significant multi-responsibility accumulation. run_conversation at ~1470 lines is a genuine monster function mixing 5+ distinct concerns. The 11 independent config.yaml parses in one file and the triplicated cache code are textbook examples of patterns the dimension rubric targets. These issues pull the score well below 80 (solid-but-uneven) into the 60s territory where design decisions routinely obscure intent in the core orchestration layer.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates strong contract discipline in its core patterns: tool handlers use a consistent JSON error envelope, dataclass serialization follows uniform to_dict/from_dict conventions, and the MemoryStore API reliably returns success/error dicts. However, there are several return-type annotation lies where the declared type does not cover all return paths (get_toolset_info returning None, _deliver_to_platform returning SendResult instead of Dict), and a cluster of cache-directory getters that silently create directories as a side effect. These issues are localized rather than systemic.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The positive patterns are pervasive and well-maintained -- the tool handler contract, dataclass serialization pattern, and error envelope convention are consistent across 20+ files. The defects (3 return-type lies, 3 getter-with-side-effects) are real but localized to specific modules. This puts the codebase solidly above the 60-mark (no significant drag on developers) but below 80 (the type annotation lies in toolsets.py and delivery.py could cause runtime surprises). Score of 74.5 reflects real but non-systemic contract violations against an otherwise disciplined backdrop.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Control flow in this codebase is generally direct and traceable. The main agent loop, gateway dispatch, and command handling follow straightforward branching without deep nesting or tangled logic. However, scattered across multiple modules are identical if/else branches that obscure intent, a dead code block with an always-false condition in checkpoint_manager, and an async function that misleadingly wraps blocking I/O. These are isolated instances rather than a systemic pattern.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase earns above-80 because a developer can mostly trace control flow without surprises, and no serious patterns of always-true conditions, dead code after returns, or boolean simplification issues exist. The score is held below 85 by four separate identical-branch patterns (telegram, uninstall, skills_sync, fuzzy_match), a dead code block with always-false condition in checkpoint_manager, duplicated dead code in cli.py, and a misleading async _send_email that blocks the event loop. These are all fixable with single-edit changes but span multiple files.

type_safety.dimension_character: The codebase shows a split personality on type safety. Well-structured gateway dataclasses and the tool registry have careful Optional[] annotations and return types. However, the two largest classes (AIAgent in run_agent.py and SessionStore/PairingStore) have pervasive Optional-without-annotation parameters, and the two most-compared discriminator values (api_mode and SessionResetPolicy.mode) use bare str instead of Literal or Enum types, making it easy for typos to silently pass. The pattern of typing parameters as X but defaulting to None recurs across at least 15 parameters in production code.
type_safety.score_rationale: The consistent 'typed as X, defaults to None' anti-pattern affects core classes that drive the entire application (AIAgent, SessionStore, PairingStore). While this pattern is tolerated by Python's runtime, it makes static analysis unreliable and contradicts the annotation's promise. The lack of Literal types for the heavily-discriminated api_mode and reset policy mode compounds the issue -- these two strings control major code paths. Strong typing in the gateway dataclasses prevents this from being worse, but the AIAgent class alone (the central orchestrator) has roughly 10 such violations. Score of 52 reflects significant but not catastrophic type discipline issues: the annotations exist but frequently lie about nullability.

issues.suggestion: Either run the blocking SMTP work in an executor (await asyncio.to_thread(_send_email_sync, ...)) or use an async SMTP library like aiosmtplib.
evidence: Line 289: _send_email is declared async but uses smtplib.SMTP (blocking I/O) for the entire operation. It is awaited by _send_to_platform, so it must be async, but the blocking SMTP calls will freeze the event loop.

issues.summary: 15+ parameters typed as concrete types (str, int, dict, callable) but default to None without Optional annotation
issues.suggestion: Add Optional[] to all parameters that accept None. For example: base_url: Optional[str] = None, last_prompt_tokens: Optional[int] = None. This is a mechanical find-and-replace that can be done per-file.
evidence: run_agent.py:215 base_url: str = None
run_agent.py:216 api_key: str = None
run_agent.py:218 api_mode: str = None
run_agent.py:242 max_tokens: int = None
run_agent.py:237 tool_progress_callback: callable = None
gateway/session.py:571 last_prompt_tokens: int = None
gateway/pairing.py:95 platform: str = None
gateway/hermes_control_client.py:55 body: dict = None

issues.summary: AIAgent.api_mode is bare str but acts as a 3-value discriminated union compared 50+ times
issues.suggestion: Define ApiMode = Literal['chat_completions', 'codex_responses', 'anthropic_messages'] and type the field as Optional[ApiMode]. This enables static analysis to catch typos in the 50+ comparison sites.
evidence: run_agent.py:218 api_mode: str = None, but only valid values are 'chat_completions', 'codex_responses', 'anthropic_messages'
run_agent.py:321 validates against a set literal but discards the constraint immediately into a str field
50+ comparisons like self.api_mode == 'codex_responses' scattered across run_agent.py with no typo protection

issues.summary: SessionResetPolicy.mode is bare str but only valid as 'daily' | 'idle' | 'both' | 'none'
issues.suggestion: Define ResetMode = Literal['daily', 'idle', 'both', 'none'] and type SessionResetPolicy.mode as ResetMode. The enum is already documented in the docstring; making it a type gives static analysis teeth.
evidence: gateway/config.py:73 mode: str = 'both' with comment listing 4 valid values
gateway/session.py:414,419,424,452,457,462 six comparisons against string literals with no protection

issues.summary: MessageEvent.source typed as SessionSource but defaults to None -- annotation lies about nullability
issues.suggestion: Change to source: Optional[SessionSource] = None. Alternatively, if source is always set before use, make it a required field and remove the default.
evidence: gateway/platforms/base.py:283 source: SessionSource = None
Callers like _process_message_background (line 706) access event.source.thread_id without null check, relying on runtime convention rather than type guarantees

issues.summary: build_channel_directory uses Dict[Any, Any] where specific types exist
issues.suggestion: Change parameter type to Dict[Platform, BasePlatformAdapter] which matches actual usage and enables static analysis of the key comparisons.
evidence: gateway/channel_directory.py:44 def build_channel_directory(adapters: Dict[Any, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]
The function immediately compares keys against Platform enum values (line 56: platform == Platform.DISCORD), proving the key type is Platform

add.description: Platform adapters import from gateway.config and gateway.session in a one-directional downward flow, while gateway.run imports platform adapters upward. This is a well-structured parent-child dependency relationship within the gateway subsystem.

add.header: Interrupt module as clean shared signaling
add.description: tools/interrupt.py encapsulates a threading.Event behind set_interrupt/is_interrupted API, providing cross-cutting interrupt signaling without exposing mutable state. Called from run_agent.py, polled by 20 tool modules.
tools/registry.py documents its intended import hierarchy in its docstring to prevent circular imports. However, the actual code violates this contract by importing _run_async from model_tools.

Root-level files (cli.py, run_agent.py) and hermes_cli/ have 24 bidirectional import edges. hermes_cli/callbacks.py and hermes_cli/main.py import back from root cli.py, creating a dependency cycle managed only by deferred imports.

30+ production files use sys.path.insert at module scope to enable cross-package imports. This indicates the project lacks proper package installation (editable install) and relies on runtime path manipulation, removing static analysis protection against boundary violations.

add.header: Three massive hub files concentrate coupling
add.description: cli.py (5162 LOC), run_agent.py (5725 LOC), and gateway/run.py (4067 LOC) collectively import from nearly every subsystem, acting as coupling magnifiers where changes in shared modules ripple through all three.
environments/tool_call_parsers/__init__.py imports all parser modules which import register_parser from __init__.py. This is a standard plugin registry pattern and does not cause runtime issues.
Major packages (agent/, gateway/, hermes_cli/, tools/, cron/, honcho_integration/) each own a single domain capability. A new engineer can predict what each directory contains from the name alone.
gateway/platforms/ follows a uniform one-file-per-platform convention with a shared base.py ABC and an ADDING_A_PLATFORM.md guide, making the extension pattern obvious.
All 30+ tools self-register via tools/registry.py at import time. model_tools.py is a thin orchestration layer. This is the single consistent architectural pattern for tool management.
gateway/__init__.py defines an explicit __all__ exporting config types, session management, and delivery routing -- a curated API surface that matches the package's stated responsibility.

cli.py (5.2K lines), run_agent.py (5.7K lines), and gateway/run.py (4K lines) each concentrate multiple responsibilities. The agent/ extraction started but left the core intact. These files are the primary source of ownership ambiguity within their domains.

hermes_cli/main.py is the argparse subcommand dispatcher ('hermes setup', 'hermes gateway', etc.) while cli.py is the interactive REPL. main.py delegates 'hermes chat' to cli.py. This is intentional but means CLI responsibilities span two locations.
The documented structure includes a nonexistent acp_adapter/ directory and omits many real directories and files, making it an unreliable map of the codebase.

batch_runner.py, trajectory_compressor.py, rl_cli.py, mini_swe_runner.py, and toolset_distributions.py are RL/research infrastructure that sit at the project root alongside production code without clear separation.
All Python modules use snake_case for functions/variables and PascalCase for classes. No camelCase drift detected across any package boundary.
Every tool file in tools/ calls registry.register() at module level. model_tools.py triggers registration by importing all tool modules. This pattern is documented and followed consistently.
All 7 platform adapters inherit BasePlatformAdapter, implement the same abstract methods, and expose a module-level check_*_requirements() function. The structural contract is reliable.
All 38 domain data types use @dataclass. No mixed TypedDict/NamedTuple usage, eliminating one common source of convention drift.
All parser classes in environments/tool_call_parsers/ inherit from ToolCallParser ABC and use the @register_parser decorator. Adding a new parser follows an obvious pattern.

telegram, discord, slack, whatsapp adapters use sys.path.insert before importing gateway modules; signal, email, homeassistant import directly. Likely reflects different authorship periods — earlier adapters added the hack, later ones found it unnecessary.

All tool handlers return JSON strings with {"error": ...} key on failure. model_tools.handle_function_call wraps uncaught exceptions in the same format. This gives the LLM a predictable error contract.
Platform adapters return SendResult with explicit success/error fields, providing structured error reporting at the adapter boundary.
Conditional imports (discord, slack_bolt, croniter) consistently use except ImportError to set sentinel flags like SLACK_AVAILABLE = False, enabling clean feature-gating.

add.header: Deliberate graceful degradation for optional subsystems
add.description: Honcho, SQLite session DB, skin engine, and memory systems are all treated as optional -- failures are caught and the agent continues. This is an intentional availability choice, not accidental error swallowing. The issue is that the catches are too broad (Exception instead of specific types).

139 instances of 'except Exception: pass' across 47 files. Most protect optional features but catch programming errors (AttributeError, TypeError) alongside expected IO/parsing failures, making bugs in optional subsystems invisible.

Error reporting uses a mix of print(), logger.debug(), logger.warning(), and logger.error() with no consistent severity mapping. Same-severity failures are logged differently in sibling functions within the same file.
hermes_cli/auth.py defines a well-structured AuthError with metadata fields. No other module defines domain-specific exceptions, so callers must catch broad types everywhere else.

Gateway subsystem uses highly specific names throughout: SessionSource, MessageEvent, SendResult, BasePlatformAdapter, HomeChannel, SessionResetPolicy. A developer reading gateway code can predict class roles from names alone.

Boolean-returning methods and properties consistently use is_/should_ prefixes: is_command(), is_connected, is_interrupted(), should_compress(), should_compress_preflight(). No name/behavior mismatches found in predicate methods.
All production code uses snake_case for functions and variables, PascalCase for classes, with zero camelCase leakage outside of unittest setUp/tearDown. This is a codebase-wide invariant.
Utility functions use verb+noun patterns that communicate intent: atomic_json_write, build_channel_directory, scan_skill_commands, build_skill_invocation_message, cache_image_from_bytes.
hermes_cli/skills_hub.py uses a do_ prefix (do_search, do_install, etc.) that diverges from the codebase's pattern of domain-qualified verb+noun names. This is a localized style island.

Each tool module calls registry.register() at import time; model_tools.py triggers discovery then delegates to the registry for schema retrieval and dispatch. This eliminates the need for a central routing table and keeps tool definitions co-located with their implementations.

BasePlatformAdapter in gateway/platforms/base.py has genuine extension pressure with 7 concrete subclasses (Telegram, Discord, WhatsApp, Slack, Signal, Email, HomeAssistant). The abstract methods (connect, disconnect, send, get_chat_info) define a minimal contract; optional methods (send_image, send_voice, edit_message) have sensible fallback defaults.
utils.py contains only atomic file write utilities (JSON and YAML) at 89 LOC. It avoids becoming a generic helper dumping ground, which is a common anti-pattern in Python projects.

The AIAgent.__init__ accumulates parameters from API config, provider routing, callbacks, session management, and feature flags without grouping into config objects. Each new feature adds another keyword argument, making the constructor progressively harder to understand and use correctly.

model_tools.py provides backward-compatible wrapper functions (get_all_tool_names, check_toolset_requirements, etc.) that forward to the registry singleton. These have 5+ active callers across cli.py, run_agent.py, doctor.py and serve as a stable import path -- an intentional facade, not unnecessary indirection.

pyproject.toml uses fine-grained optional extras ([messaging], [cron], [honcho], [mcp], [pty], etc.) to keep platform-specific and heavy dependencies out of the core install. This lets users install only what they need.

Optional packages like croniter, elevenlabs, simple_term_menu, and fal_client are imported at call sites with try/except and graceful fallbacks, avoiding ImportError for users who don't need those features.
The project has no pip lock file or constraints file. Dependencies use version floors (>=) but no upper bounds or exact pins, relying on virtual environments for reproducibility.

add.header: Dual HTTP client libraries in core
add.description: Both requests and httpx are core dependencies. httpx dominates (66+ sites) while requests is used in only 2 files (6 calls). This appears to be organic drift rather than a deliberate split by sync/async need.

Small modules like tools/interrupt.py, utils.py, gateway/hermes_control_client.py, and gateway/control_api.py have focused, proportionate internals with flat control flow. These modules demonstrate what good low-level craft looks like in this codebase.
ReadResult, WriteResult, PatchResult, MessageEvent, SendResult all follow the same dataclass pattern with optional fields and to_dict() methods, giving a predictable shape to function returns.
tools/fuzzy_match.py defines matching strategies as a list of (name, function) tuples iterated in a single loop, keeping control flow flat despite 8 different matching algorithms.

The central message processing method in gateway/run.py exceeds 700 lines with deeply nested inline logic for session hygiene, media enrichment, and transcript persistence -- features that were added incrementally without extraction.

gateway/run.py contains 11 separate inline config.yaml load-parse-extract sequences with identical try/except patterns, a consequence of each _load_* method being self-contained rather than sharing a cached loader.

BasePlatformAdapter -> MessageEvent -> SendResult provides an explicit, minimal contract with normalized data types. All 7 platform adapters produce the same MessageEvent shape, and the gateway consumes them uniformly through set_message_handler().

ControlAPI (server) and HermesControl (client) form a clean REST boundary. The client uses only stdlib (urllib), the server has a focused 5-endpoint surface. External tools interact without coupling to gateway internals.

add.header: Tool registry as single registration seam
add.description: tools/registry.py provides a single ToolEntry registration point. Tools self-register at import time via registry.register(), and model_tools.py provides unified get_definitions/dispatch. No parallel data structures needed by consumers.

add.header: Hook system with isolated error propagation
add.description: HookRegistry.emit() fires lifecycle events with catch-and-log error isolation, transparent sync/async handler support, and wildcard matching. Hooks never block the main pipeline -- a predictable error propagation pattern at this boundary.

20+ independent yaml.safe_load() calls in gateway/run.py each open, parse, and extract from config.yaml independently. Config translation at boundaries happens in 20 different places instead of one obvious place, creating glue-code entropy.

The core message pipeline is a 700+ line method mixing authorization, command dispatch, media enrichment, session hygiene, agent invocation, and transcript persistence inline. Orchestration does not read as composition of collaborators.

Temporary AIAgent instances for compression, memory flush, and session hygiene are constructed in 4+ places with varying parameters (different max_iterations, toolsets, model resolution). The handoff pattern at the gateway->agent boundary is not consistent.
All tests get auto-isolated HERMES_HOME (tmp_path) and a 30-second SIGALRM timeout via autouse fixtures in conftest.py, preventing state pollution and test hangs.

Only 4 of ~150 test files reference snapshots. Tests assert on behavioral outcomes (status codes, method calls, state changes) rather than output snapshots, making them resilient to formatting changes.

Gateway tests are organized per-slash-command (test_autoreply_command.py, test_background_command.py, etc.) rather than per-module. This gives good feature coverage but leaves the central _handle_message orchestration pipeline undertested.

add.header: Fake HA server for integration testing
add.description: tests/fakes/fake_ha_server.py provides a real HTTP+WebSocket server mimicking the Home Assistant API, enabling realistic integration tests without external dependencies.

add.header: object.__new__ pattern for GatewayRunner test construction
add.description: 9 test files bypass GatewayRunner.__init__ via object.__new__ and manually set internal attributes. This creates fragile coupling — any new attribute requires updating all 9 files.

gateway/run.py (4067 LOC), tools/terminal_tool.py (1266 LOC), hermes_cli/main.py (3149 LOC), and hermes_cli/auth.py (2092 LOC) are among the largest and most-imported modules but have no dedicated test files.

All 8 platform adapters (Telegram, Discord, WhatsApp, Slack, Signal, Email, Home Assistant, plus the abstract base) share identical send/connect/disconnect signatures. A developer seeing one adapter can predict the shape of all others.
The ToolRegistry.register() method provides a single registration interface that all 20+ tool files use consistently. Schema, handler, check_fn, and metadata follow the same shape everywhere.
HermesControl (the client) is entirely synchronous using stdlib urllib, while ControlAPI (the server) is entirely async using aiohttp. No mixed sync/async within either module's public API.

SessionSource, SessionEntry, HomeChannel, PlatformConfig, DeliveryTarget all follow the same to_dict/from_dict class method pattern for serialization, making the API predictable across the gateway subsystem.

cache_document_from_bytes takes a filename parameter where cache_image_from_bytes and cache_audio_from_bytes take an ext parameter. The document variant legitimately needs richer naming but breaks the sibling pattern without explanation.

All platform message handling routes through _is_user_authorized() in gateway/run.py, providing a single enforcement point with a consistent multi-layer chain: per-platform allow-all, pairing store, platform allowlist, global allowlist, global allow-all, default deny.

gateway/pairing.py implements cryptographic code generation, rate limiting, lockout after failed attempts, code expiry (1 hour), and restrictive file permissions (0600). This provides a secure onboarding path for new users without requiring static ID configuration.

add.header: Control API scoped to localhost with opt-in
add.description: The HTTP control API binds to 127.0.0.1 only and requires HERMES_CONTROL_API=true to activate. Injected messages re-enter the auth pipeline, so the control surface does not bypass user-level authorization.

add.header: Platform allowlist env vars as magic strings
add.description: Env var names like TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS are repeated as string literals across run.py, setup.py, gateway.py, config.py, and migration scripts. The enforcement point is centralized so drift risk is low, but adding a new platform requires updating strings in multiple files.

Module docstrings in agent/, gateway/, tools/registry.py, and utility modules explain architecture, resolution chains, import ordering rationale, and non-obvious constraints -- genuinely useful context that aids navigation.

add.header: Purposeful logging with no noisy entry/exit pattern
add.description: logger.getLogger(__name__) is used pervasively but logging calls are targeted at errors and key lifecycle events. No entry/exit debug logging or full object dumps observed.

add.header: Section dividers as navigation aids in large files
add.description: Consistent use of # === / # --- / # -- dividers in files like run_agent.py, hermes_state.py, tools/registry.py provides visual structure without ceremony.

add.header: Docstring bloat concentrated in toolsets modules
add.description: toolsets.py and toolset_distributions.py have verbose multi-section docstrings on trivial 1-2 line functions, contrasting with the rest of the codebase where docstrings are proportionate to function complexity.

add.header: Duplicated bootstrap boilerplate across entry points
add.description: The .env loading pattern (try utf-8, except latin-1 fallback) plus MSWEA env setup is copied verbatim across 7+ files rather than extracted to a shared helper.

add.header: Legacy toolset names well-documented and tested
add.description: _LEGACY_TOOLSET_MAP in model_tools.py provides backward compat for old _tools-suffixed names. It has dedicated tests (TestLegacyToolsetMap) and clear usage in get_tool_definitions(). This is an intentional compat layer with active callers.

add.header: Config normalization handles legacy keys cleanly
add.description: _normalize_max_turns_config and _normalize_recall_mode are clean one-directional transforms that silently upgrade old config formats. They run on every config load without side effects.

Session transcripts are written to both SQLite and JSONL. Reads prefer SQLite with JSONL fallback. The write side has not been migrated to SQLite-only, leaving indefinite dual-write overhead. Comment says 'during transition' but no completion criteria exist.

add.header: Deprecated env vars with migration path
add.description: HERMES_TOOL_PROGRESS and HERMES_TOOL_PROGRESS_MODE are deprecated in favor of config.yaml. The config migration system auto-converts them, but gateway/run.py still reads the old env var as a fallback, keeping the deprecated path alive.

add.header: OpenClaw migration is a proper optional skill
add.description: The openclaw-migration skill is structured under optional-skills/ with its own CLI entry points (hermes claw migrate), comprehensive tests, and preset system. This is a well-bounded, intentional migration tool rather than leftover shim code.

The top-level packages (agent/, gateway/, hermes_cli/, tools/, honcho_integration/, cron/, environments/) map cleanly to distinct functional domains with minimal ambiguity. A developer can predict which top-level package owns a given concern.
gateway/platforms/ demonstrates good subdirectory design — one file per platform adapter plus a base.py abstract class, with clean coupling back to gateway/ parent.

add.header: Core root files are legitimately core
add.description: run_agent.py (fan_in=24), cli.py (fan_in=15), model_tools.py (fan_in=14), hermes_constants.py (fan_in=11), hermes_state.py (fan_in=11), toolsets.py (fan_in=9) all exceed the fan_in >= 5 threshold and are imported across multiple subdirectories — they belong at root.

The tools/ directory has grown to 39 files with identifiable subgroups (5 skills files, 8+ infrastructure files) mixed alongside tool implementations, making navigation harder than necessary for its size.
hermes_cli/ has 26 files mixing CLI subcommands, configuration management, display/theming, and auth infrastructure in a single flat directory with no subdirectories.

Five peripheral root files (batch_runner, trajectory_compressor, mini_swe_runner, rl_cli, toolset_distributions) share a batch/training pipeline concern but live at the root with low fan-in (0-3), diluting root-level navigability.

tools/registry.py has no imports from model_tools or tool files; tool files import from registry; model_tools imports both. This three-layer chain is documented in the module docstring and prevents circular imports by construction.
hermes_constants.py is explicitly designed with no dependencies, providing URL constants that any module can import without risk of boot-order issues or circular imports.

Expensive clients (Firecrawl, Honcho, auxiliary OpenAI) use module-level None sentinels with get-or-create functions, deferring network connections and credential resolution until first use rather than import time.
Timezone resolution does file I/O (config.yaml) but is deferred to first call of now() and cached, with an explicit reset_cache() for tests. Good pattern for config that requires I/O.

add.header: Gateway sys.path manipulation at import time
add.description: Six gateway platform modules and gateway/run.py each call sys.path.insert(0, ...) at module scope to find the project root. This creates duplicate sys.path entries and couples import behavior to filesystem structure rather than proper packaging.

add.header: Entry-point environment mutation at module scope
add.description: cli.py and gateway/run.py load .env files and mutate os.environ at module scope rather than inside their main() functions. Tests importing these modules inherit environment side effects.

add.header: Clean tool and parser registry patterns
add.description: tools/registry.py and environments/tool_call_parsers/__init__.py both use decorator-based registration with ABC base classes, providing focused, data-driven extensibility. These demonstrate the team's capacity for disciplined design.

The agent/ package extracts prompt_builder, context_compressor, display, trajectory, model_metadata, and anthropic_adapter into focused modules with clear single responsibilities and no circular dependencies.

add.header: Three orchestration monoliths dominate the codebase
add.description: run_agent.py (5725 LOC), cli.py (5162 LOC), and gateway/run.py (4067 LOC) together comprise ~15K lines and mix configuration loading, session management, command dispatch, agent lifecycle, and UI concerns within single files/classes.

Seven _load_* static methods and four inline blocks each independently open and parse the same YAML file. This is a structural pattern that should load once and distribute, not a single-responsibility decomposition.

All tool handlers (30+) return JSON strings with either {success: true, ...} or {error: '...'} pattern. This convention is followed uniformly across memory_tool, file_tools, browser_tool, terminal_tool, and others, giving callers a reliable contract.

SessionSource, SessionEntry, SessionContext, HomeChannel, PlatformConfig, and SessionResetPolicy all implement to_dict/from_dict classmethods with matching type annotations. Round-trip fidelity is maintained across all six dataclasses.

add.header: Return type annotations in toolsets.py inconsistent
add.description: get_toolset_info returns Optional[Dict] but is annotated as Dict. get_toolset correctly uses Optional. The inconsistency suggests annotations were added piecemeal rather than reviewed systematically.

add.header: Cache dir getters have hidden mkdir side effects
add.description: Three get_*_cache_dir() functions in base.py create directories on every call. The 'get' prefix hides the mutation, though the side effect is idempotent and harmless in practice.
AST scan of all production files found zero instances of unreachable code after unconditional return or raise statements at function body level, indicating disciplined control flow termination.
No if x: return True else: return False patterns or return True if x else False ternaries found in production code. Boolean logic is expressed directly.
Four separate files contain if/else constructs where both branches execute identical code, likely from iterative development where a planned distinction was never implemented.

add.header: Async-without-await in tool handlers by registry contract
add.description: rl_training_tool.py declares 9 async functions that never await, registered with is_async=True. This is framework-driven (the tool dispatcher uses await) but means these functions are synchronous code wrapped in async, adding coroutine overhead without benefit.

add.header: Clean control flow in main dispatch paths
add.description: The gateway message dispatch, agent conversation loop, and command handling all use straightforward if/elif chains and early returns. A developer can trace message flow without encountering surprising branches or convoluted logic.

add.header: Gateway dataclasses have thorough Optional annotations
add.description: SessionSource, SessionEntry, SessionContext, PlatformConfig, and HomeChannel use Optional[] correctly for nullable fields and have complete return type annotations on all methods. This forms a well-typed core for the session management layer.

The tool registry public API (register, dispatch, get_definitions, query helpers) and BasePlatformAdapter abstract interface have explicit parameter and return type annotations, including proper Optional usage.

add.header: Pervasive X-typed-None-default pattern in AIAgent and stores
add.description: AIAgent.__init__ has ~10 parameters typed as str/int/callable that default to None without Optional. SessionStore.update_session, PairingStore methods, and HermesControl._request repeat the pattern. This undermines static analysis across the main orchestration class.

add.header: String-typed discriminated unions for api_mode and reset mode
add.description: AIAgent.api_mode (3 values, 50+ comparisons) and SessionResetPolicy.mode (4 values, 6 comparisons) use bare str instead of Literal or Enum. These are the two most safety-critical discriminators in the codebase, controlling API dispatch and session lifecycle.

add.header: Type-ignore comments are minimal and explained
add.description: Only 4 type-ignore comments in production code, all on conditional imports (aiohttp, backports.zoneinfo, anthropic SDK) where the pattern is standard for optional dependencies.
